name: Social Media Personality Inference (Kosinski et al., myPersonality, language-based assessment)
core_claim: This is not a personality instrument but a method: inferring the Big Five (OCEAN) traits passively from a person's digital footprint — Facebook Likes, posts, status-update language, tweets — instead of asking them to fill in a questionnaire. The seminal claim (Kosinski, Stillwell & Graepel, PNAS 2013) is that a sparse, easily accessible behavioural trace (Likes) predicts not only Big Five personality but a wide range of sensitive attributes (sexual orientation, ethnicity, religion, political views, intelligence, substance use, parental-separation history). The myPersonality dataset (~6M users, ~3M of whom granted profile access and took validated psychometric tests) was the engine behind this line of work and behind the language-based-assessment (LBA) tradition (Park et al. 2014; Schwartz et al. 2013 open-vocabulary work). The relevance for media preference is the same theoretical link as for the Big Five generally (Uses-and-Gratifications / Mood-Management — stable traits shape sought gratifications), but with a crucial twist: the SIGNAL is harvested rather than elicited, so the question is not 'does personality predict media' but 'can we accurately recover personality from data the user already produces, and may we even do so'. For HelpME2C the load-bearing question is whether implicit inference could replace or supplement an onboarding quiz — and the answer is dominated by accuracy ceilings and, more decisively, by GDPR.
predictive_validity_for_media: Two separable accuracy questions must not be conflated. (1) Accuracy of inferring personality from social-media behaviour. Kosinski et al. (2013) reported Like-based prediction of Big Five with average accuracy r≈0.30 across the five domains, with Openness the best-predicted (r≈0.43) and the rest lower (~0.29-0.30) — note Openness is the strongest BOTH to infer AND the most media-relevant, which is convenient. The famous Youyou/Kosinski/Stillwell (2015 PNAS) result is a comparison, not a higher correlation: with ~300 Likes the model's self-other agreement exceeded a spouse's; with 10 Likes it beat a colleague, 70 a friend, 150 a family member. Language-based assessment converges with self-report at average r≈0.38 (Park et al. 2014, n=66,732 train / 4,824 test Facebook users; 6-month test-retest r≈0.70), and open-vocabulary methods beat closed-vocabulary (LIWC) ones. Recent LLM-based inference is LOWER, not higher: average r≈0.29 (range 0.22-0.33) between LLM-inferred and self-reported traits, with documented bias (more accurate for women and younger users). (2) How well the INFERRED trait then predicts media preference — this inherits the small Big-Five-to-genre effects (mostly |r|<0.30, Openness strongest; see big-five-neo-pi result, Rentfrow & Goldberg 2011). The two stages multiply: an inferred-Openness signal at r≈0.43 feeding a trait-to-preference link at r≈0.2-0.3 yields a very weak end-to-end media predictor. Critically, Sumpter's reanalysis argues the real-world targeting value was overstated — the personality signal recoverable from realistically available data was too coarse to drive individual-level targeting. No study validates inferred-personality-from-social-media against actual joint TV/streaming watch behaviour. [uncertain]
instrument_design: There is no 'instrument' in the questionnaire sense — that is the whole point of this approach. The 'design' is a pipeline: collect a behavioural corpus (Likes / posts / text), reduce dimensionality (SVD on the user×Like matrix in the 2013 work), and regress onto Big Five domain scores trained against myPersonality ground-truth questionnaires (typically the IPIP-NEO 100- or 20-item taken inside the app). Modern variants fine-tune a pretrained language model (BERT-family) on text→trait regression, or prompt an LLM zero/few-shot. Input requirements are non-trivial: the 2013 accuracy curve needs on the order of tens-to-hundreds of Likes; LBA needs a substantial text history (Park used users with adequate status-update volume). There is NO sub-2-minute, 6-10-item version because there is no elicitation step at all — instead there is a data-access requirement (OAuth scope to the user's Likes/posts, or pasted text). For a low-friction onboarding context the relevant 'minimum viable' framing is: minimum data the user must connect or paste to get a usable Openness estimate — and that floor (tens of Likes or a few hundred words of self-authored text) is both high-friction to obtain honestly and legally fraught to request. The myPersonality dataset itself was retired/closed to new researchers in 2018 after the Cambridge Analytica fallout, so it is no longer an accessible training resource.
cold_start_applicability: Superficially attractive for cold start — it promises a personality prior with ZERO explicit quiz effort — but in practice it is a poor fit for HelpME2C's actual cold start. The user with no VIEWING history is exactly the user we are onboarding; but this method needs a different history (a rich social-media footprint) which (a) we do not have, (b) requires a third-party OAuth connection (Facebook/X) that is itself friction and a Stop-and-Ask external-ingress/privacy decision, and (c) is decreasingly available as platforms lock down Like/post APIs post-2018. Signal quality before first interaction is at best the multiplied-weak chain described above, and worse than a 4-6 item explicit Openness/Extraversion quiz that takes the same 60-90s and requires no data-sharing. It does support cross-medium inference in principle (medium-agnostic trait link), but provides no advantage over directly eliciting the trait. Bottom line: for cold start, ASK don't INFER — explicit short-form elicitation (see big-five-short-forms result) dominates passive inference on accuracy-per-friction, data-availability, and especially legal exposure. The only context where passive inference wins is when the user genuinely refuses any quiz but will connect an account — a narrow, legally heavy edge case. [uncertain]
group_compatibility_use: No special advantage over explicit Big Five for the two-person case, and an extra problem. The downstream aggregation logic is identical to the Big Five result: convert each person's (inferred) trait vector into a content prior, then aggregate the two priors with a group-recommender strategy — Least-Misery-with-a-floor as the recommended default for a 'show both want to watch' North Star, Average as the alternative, with optional personality-weighted influence (cf. arXiv:2304.08851). The added problem is COMPOUNDING ERROR: each partner's trait estimate now carries inference noise (r≈0.3-0.43 recovery) ON TOP of the small trait-to-preference effect, so a two-person intersection of two noisy priors is noisier still and more likely to mislead the joint recommendation than two cleanly elicited short-form profiles. There is also an asymmetry/consent hazard: in a dyad, inferring one partner's personality from THEIR social data to recommend to BOTH means processing one person's special-category-adjacent data partly for another's benefit — a consent and fairness complication that explicit, per-user, opt-in elicitation avoids entirely. Evidence on which combination predicts JOINT media satisfaction remains thin regardless of how traits were obtained. [uncertain] Recommendation: if personality is used in group mode at all, elicit it; do not infer it.
quiz_ux_implications: The defining UX property is that this approach AVOIDS a quiz — and that is both its pitch and its trap. Pros: no acquiescence bias, no social-desirability bias, no faking (you cannot game a quiz you do not take), no Likert fatigue; the signal is 'behavioural' and arguably more honest. Cons that dominate in 2026: (1) the data-connection step (connect Facebook/X, or paste your posts) is HIGHER friction and HIGHER creepiness than a 6-item poster A/B quiz — users perceive 'we read your social media to guess your personality' as invasive (the Cambridge Analytica association is now mainstream and toxic); (2) post-2018 API restrictions mean the rich Like/post data the method needs is mostly no longer obtainable through sanctioned APIs; (3) it surfaces a transparency problem — under GDPR you must tell the user you are inferring personality and let them object, which converts the 'frictionless' pitch into an explicit consent dialogue anyway. Net: the perceived-honesty advantage does not survive contact with the trust, friction, and legal-disclosure costs. A short disguised/gamified explicit quiz is both higher-trust and higher-signal-per-second here.
cross_cultural_validity: Weaker and more biased than explicit Big Five short forms for HelpME2C's multilingual catalog. The inference models were trained overwhelmingly on English-language, US/UK, younger, Facebook-using samples (myPersonality skewed Western and English). Language-based assessment is intrinsically language-bound — an open-vocabulary model trained on English status updates does not transfer to Korean, Japanese, Arabic or Hindi text without retraining on matched localised corpora, which largely do not exist at myPersonality scale. Documented demographic bias (more accurate for women and younger users) compounds the Openness-is-translation-fragile problem already noted for the Big Five. LLM-based inference inherits the cultural and linguistic biases of its pretraining distribution. So the method degrades precisely in the non-Western, non-English locales HelpME2C must serve, on top of the construct-level Openness-congruence weak spots (Schmitt et al. 2007: lowest Openness congruence in some African/South-Asian samples). For a multilingual product this is a strong mark against passive inference relative to professionally localised explicit items. [uncertain]
privacy_consent_implications: This is the decisive field and the reason to be very cautious. Inferring personality from social-media data is the textbook GDPR profiling case, and the Kosinski/Cambridge-Analytica lineage makes it reputationally radioactive. Three layers: (1) Lawful basis — inference is 'profiling' under Art. 4(4) and processing the source data needs an Art. 6 basis; for a voluntary product, explicit consent is the only defensible one, and it cannot be bundled into ToS (the Cambridge Analytica 'This Is Your Digital Life' app and its friends-data API loophole is the canonical example of consent done illegally). (2) Special-category risk (Art. 9) — Kosinski 2013 is the proof that this exact technique reliably infers sexual orientation (88%), ethnicity (95%), political affiliation (85%), and religion from the SAME footprint used for personality; under ICO/EDPB guidance, if you process data from which you can or do infer special-category attributes, you risk falling under Art. 9 and needing explicit consent plus a special-category condition. A Neuroticism estimate is additionally inferable to mental health. (3) Transparency & objection — Arts. 13-14, 21, 22 require disclosing the inference, its logic, and a right to object; profiling without meaningful consent is precisely what GDPR's anti-profiling provisions target. Practical conclusion for HelpME2C: this is a hard Stop-and-Ask / ADR-0012 zone; it adds new external ingress (OAuth to a social platform) AND processes special-category-adjacent inferred data AND processes user data via an automated profiling step — three separate confirmation triggers. The privacy cost vastly exceeds the marginal accuracy benefit over an explicit short-form quiz. Recommend NOT collecting or inferring from social-media footprints.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: The theme-vocabulary mapping is identical to the Big Five result because the OUTPUT is the same OCEAN vector — only the acquisition method differs. High inferred-Openness (the best-recovered trait, r≈0.43) -> nonlinear-storytelling, slow-burn, magical-realism, meditation-on-time, meditation-on-death, character-study, satire-of-power, absurdist-comedy, cosmic-horror, alternate-history, first-contact, dystopian-society, and tolerance for foreign-language/art-house content; low Openness -> whodunit, workplace-procedural, heist-and-caper, underdog-rise, clean redemption-arc. High Extraversion -> ensemble-mosaic, found-family, friendship-tested, chase-and-escape, celebrity-and-fame. High Neuroticism -> grief-and-loss, loneliness-and-isolation, trauma-survival, mental-illness-portrayal, existential-horror (with care). High Agreeableness -> found-family, platonic-soulmates, mentor-and-pupil, coming-of-age. High Conscientiousness -> workplace-procedural, mystery-investigation, institutional themes, higher long-form completion. The important caveat unique to this method: because the trait estimate is itself noisy (r≈0.3-0.43 recovery), these directional mappings are even WEAKER than for elicited Big Five and should not drive per-title ranking — at most a faint diversity/tie-break tilt. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: EXCLUDE for HelpME2C Phase 1A, with a high bar to ever reconsider. Reasons, in order of weight: (1) Privacy/legal — passive personality inference from social-media data is GDPR profiling over special-category-adjacent inferred data plus new external OAuth ingress; it trips at least three Stop-and-Ask triggers and ADR-0012, and carries the toxic Cambridge Analytica association that directly undermines the trust HelpME2C needs. (2) Data availability — post-2018 platform API lockdowns mean the rich Like/post footprints the method needs are mostly no longer obtainable through sanctioned APIs. (3) Accuracy-per-friction — the connect/paste step is HIGHER friction and creepier than a 6-item quiz, while end-to-end media-prediction accuracy is LOWER (inference noise multiplies the already-small trait-to-genre effect). (4) Cross-cultural — degrades exactly in the non-English locales the catalog must serve. The correct design is the inverse of this approach: ASK for the trait explicitly via a 4-6 item gamified short form (see big-five-short-forms and big-five-neo-pi results) rather than INFER it. What would have to change to revisit: a future, fully-consented, on-device or transient-inference architecture that (a) never stores raw footprint or special-category inferences, (b) only outputs a derived content-prior vector, (c) has cleared an Art. 9 review, AND (d) demonstrably beats explicit elicitation on accuracy — a high and currently unmet bar. Use the literature as EVIDENCE (it proves personality IS recoverable and IS media-relevant, justifying an explicit quiz) rather than as a method to deploy. North-Star trace: the explicit-elicitation alternative this points to is what actually serves 'two people discover a show they both want to watch on day one'; the passive-inference method does not, and adds legal risk.
key_sources: Kosinski, M., Stillwell, D., & Graepel, T. (2013). 'Private traits and attributes are predictable from digital records of human behavior.' PNAS 110(15):5802-5805 — https://www.pnas.org/doi/full/10.1073/pnas.1218772110 (FREE full text + PDF). Youyou, W., Kosinski, M., & Stillwell, D. (2015). 'Computer-based personality judgments are more accurate than those made by humans.' PNAS 112(4):1036-1040 (FREE via PMC). Park, G., Schwartz, H.A., Eichstaedt, J.C. et al. (2014/2015). 'Automatic Personality Assessment Through Social Media Language.' Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 108(6):934-952 — PDF: https://sites.socsci.uci.edu/~lpearl/courses/readings/ParkEtAl2014_AutomaticPersonalityAssessment.pdf (FREE preprint). Schwartz, H.A. et al. (2013). 'Personality, Gender, and Age in the Language of Social Media: The Open-Vocabulary Approach.' PLoS ONE (FREE). LLM inference: 'Large language models can infer psychological dispositions of social media users.' PNAS Nexus 2024 — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC11211928/ (FREE). Critical reanalysis: David Sumpter, 'Why the Facebook data available to Cambridge Analytica could not be used to target personalities...' — https://soccermatics.medium.com/why-the-facebook-data-available-to-cambridge-analytica-could-not-be-used-to-target-personalities-in-2904fa0571bd (FREE). Survey: Farnadi et al., 'Computational Personality Recognition in Social Media' — http://faculty.washington.edu/mdecock/papers/gfarnadi2016a.pdf (FREE). GDPR: ICO special category / inferred data guidance — https://ico.org.uk/for-organisations/uk-gdpr-guidance-and-resources/lawful-basis/a-guide-to-lawful-basis/special-category-data/ (FREE); GDPR Arts. 4, 6, 9, 13-14, 21-22. Note: the myPersonality dataset was closed to new academic access in 2018.
